title: Electron-Phonon Coupling and Charge Density Wave Instabilities in W2N and Halogen-Functionalized W2N Monolayers
abstract: The interplay between charge-density-wave (CDW) order and superconductivity is a central problem in condensed-matter physics because both phenomena often originate from the same electron-phonon coupling (EPC) mechanism. Here, we investigate the structural, electronic, vibrational, and superconducting properties of monolayer W2N and halogen-functionalized W2N (W2NF2 and W2NCl2) using first-principles calculations. Pristine W2N exhibits pronounced phonon instabilities near the M and K points driven by exceptionally strong EPC associated with softened low-frequency phonons. The coincidence between phonon softening and enhanced phonon linewidths identifies the instability as EPC-driven and indicative of a CDW tendency. Inclusion of van der Waals interactions stabilizes the lattice and yields strong-coupling superconductivity with λ = 1.00 and Tc = 13.2 K, while fluorination further weakens the soft-phonon anomaly, resulting in a moderate-coupling superconductor with λ = 0.67 and Tc = 5.3 K. In contrast, W2NCl2 exhibits a re-emergence of CDW-related phonon softening that can be continuously suppressed by compressive strain or electron doping. Under -3% compressive strain, the EPC constant decreases from λ = 1.35 to λ = 0.71, giving rise to superconductivity with Tc = 5.8 K. Across the entire W2N family, the low-energy physics is governed by softened ZA phonons near the M point, establishing a unified framework in which CDW order and superconductivity emerge as competing manifestations of the same soft-phonon-driven EPC mechanism.
summary: This theoretical study explores how CDW instabilities and superconductivity compete in W2N monolayers. Using first-principles calculations, the authors show that both phenomena stem from the same soft-phonon-driven Electron-Phonon Coupling (EPC). Tuning the material via halogenation, strain, or doping allows researchers to tune the system between these competing quantum ground states.
main_problem: The paper investigates the fundamental competition and interplay between Charge-Density-Wave (CDW) instabilities and conventional superconductivity in monolayer transition metal nitrides.
main_result: The low-energy physics across the W2N family is governed by soft-phonon-driven Electron-Phonon Coupling (EPC), establishing CDW order and superconductivity as competing manifestations of the same mechanism.
method: The study employs first-principles Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations, utilizing Density Functional Perturbation Theory (DFPT) to analyze phonon spectra, EPC constants, and estimate superconducting critical temperatures via the Allen-Dynes modified McMillan equation.
model_or_system: The primary systems are monolayer W2N and its halogen-functionalized derivatives (W2NF2 and W2NCl2). The analysis compares pristine, van der Waals corrected, and strained/doped versions of these 2D materials.
key_observables:
Phonon instabilities (softening near M/K points), phonon linewidths ($\gamma_{q
u}$), EPC constant ($\lambda$), and superconducting critical temperature ($T_c$).
important_parameters: Compressive strain (e.g., -3%), electron doping level, and the strength of van der Waals interactions.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on DFT approximations (GGA+PBE) and the Allen-Dynes formalism for superconductivity.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate crystal structures, electronic band structures, phonon dispersions, and the evolution of soft-mode frequencies under external tuning like strain or doping.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the structural and electronic properties of the W2N family, diagnosing CDW tendencies via phonon instabilities, quantifying EPC, and calculating $T_c$ for various doping and strain regimes to establish a unified physical picture.
why_interesting: While focused on solid-state physics, the concept of competing ground states (CDW vs. SC) driven by lattice fluctuations and electron interactions is a core theme in many-body physics, relevant to understanding emergent quantum phases.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Crystal structures of pristine W2N and functionalized W2NT2 (T = F, Cl) monolayers belonging to the trigonal P¯3m1 (No. 164) space group. (a) Side and (b) top views of pristine W2N. The structural parameter h denotes the vertical separation between the N and W atomic planes. (c) Side and (d) top views of the func- tionalized W2NT2 monolayers. The parameters h1 and h2 represent the vertical distances between the N layer and the W layer, and be- tween the W layer and the terminating halogen layer, respectively. Gray, orange, and yellow spheres correspond to W, N, and halogen atoms, respectively. The primitive unit cell is outlined by solid black lines.

FIG. 2. (a) Orbital-projected electronic band structures, Fermi surfaces, and electronic density of states (EDOS) of monolayer W2N calculated without and with van der Waals (vdW) corrections. The states near the Fermi level are dominated by W-d orbitals, confirming metallic behavior. The Fermi-surface topology is preserved upon inclusion of vdW interactions, although the hole pocket around the M point becomes larger. (b) Phonon dispersions of W2N calculated with electronic smearing parameters σ = 0.02 and 0.10 Ry, showing stabilization of the soft phonon modes at larger smearing. (c) Phonon dispersions and phonon linewidths γqν for the non-vdW and vdW structures. The non- vdW phase exhibits...

FIG. 3. (a) Orbital-projected electronic band structure of monolayer W2NF2. The size of the circles is proportional to the contribution of the corresponding W-d orbitals. Multiple bands cross the Fermi level, confirming the metallic nature of the system. The low-energy electronic states are dominated by W-dz2, dxz/yz, and dx2−y2/dxy orbitals. (b) EPC-weighted phonon dispersion of W2NF2, where the color scale represents the mode-resolved electron–phonon coupling strength λqν. A pronounced low-frequency soft phonon is observed around the M point, primarily associated with the ZA (ν = 1) branch. The right panel shows the Eliashberg spectral function α2F(ω) and cumulative EPC constant λ(ω).

FIG. 4. (a) Orbital-projected electronic band structures and EDOS of pristine W2NCl2 and W2NCl2 under −3% compressive strain. Circle sizes are proportional to the corresponding W-d orbital weights. (b) Phonon dispersions and phonon linewidth γqν of pristine and strained W2NCl2. The right panel shows the Eliashberg spectral function α2F(ω) and cumulative EPC constant λ(ω) for the strained phase. (c) Evolution of the soft-mode frequency ωqCDW,ν = 1 under biaxial strain and electron doping. The soft mode is progressively hardened and becomes dynamically stable beyond the critical strain or carrier concentration.
